channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Pakistan’s High-Wire Act Gets Riskier
item.description: The country has sent troops and arms to Saudi Arabia at a delicate moment in the Iran war.

item.title: Ukraine and Russia Are Souring on U.S. Negotiations
item.description: The two nations, seemingly frustrated by the United States as a mediator, are sizing up alternatives.

item.title: Mass Protests Plunge Bolivia Into Political Upheaval
item.description: The United States has called the weekslong crisis an “ongoing coup d’état.”

item.title: Google is dethroning OpenAI as the king of consumer AI
item.description: But its users are burning through quadrillions of tokens a month

item.title: Trump’s War Is Wrecking Trump’s Economy
item.description: Inflation is rising and fault lines are widening on disruptions from history’s biggest energy shock.

item.title: Mali Strikes Rebel-Held Kidal as Insurgency Worsens
item.description: How long can the country—and the Sahel’s other junta-led governments—hold on?

item.title: Economics lessons from Home Depot
item.description: What the world’s biggest DIY store says about American housing

item.title: Donald Trump is pushing towards the end game in Cuba
item.description: With charges against Raúl Castro, his pressure campaign looks ever more like the one that deposed Nicolás Maduro

item.title: The UAE’s Syrian Gambit
item.description: To thwart competitors and secure new transport corridors, Abu Dhabi has set its sights on Damascus.

item.title: Breakthroughs for batteries could soon make them much better
item.description: Solid-state cells would be faster and safer than today’s lithium-ion equivalents

item.title: The hantavirus outbreak is a tragedy—and a valuable data source
item.description: The risk to public health remains low

item.title: How China quietly helps Russia in Ukraine
item.description: Its firms send drones, nitrocellulose for rockets and more

item.title: A Cold Shower for the AI Mania
item.description: Although generative AI tools have improved rapidly and now outperform humans across many tasks, the market's current euphoria may not be justified. With AI firms increasingly resorting to debt financing, it is worth pausing to consider all the things that could go wrong.

item.title: Has Taiwan Made Itself Immune to American Betrayal?
item.description: Although Donald Trump's recent statements about Taiwan seemed like a gift to Chinese President Xi Jinping, such rhetoric no longer tells us as much as it once did. The durability of US-Taiwan relations now rests less on individual leaders than on institutional ties and economic integration, and both have been deepening.

item.title: The Duet of Command: Key Operational Issues for OPCON Transfer
item.description: The question is no longer whether to transfer OPCON, but how to design the alliance’s command structure and capabilities so that the post-transfer combined defense posture is demonstrably more efficient and more powerful.

item.title: India’s GDP Revisions Explained: What Changed and Why it Matters
item.description: India has revised its methodology for measuring economic growth after a decade. Better calculations matter, but the real test lies in how policymakers interpret them, and turn them into decisions that shape everyday life.

item.title: India Just Signed Its Most Consequential Chip Deal. The Hard Part Starts Now.
item.description: The Tata-ASML MoU puts India inside the world’s most exclusive technology partnership, but Beijing still controls the inputs used to make chips.

item.title: Australia’s Submarine Problems
item.description: In 2009, the Australian government recognized the need to replace its aging Collins-class submarines. What followed has been a mess of indecision, changing plans, and squandered opportunities.

item.title: Operationalizing Indo-Pacific Defense and Deterrence
item.description: Insights from Kimberly Lehn.

item.title: Why Trump Sued Himself
item.description: President Donald Trump has dropped his $10 billion suit against the Internal Revenue Service over the leak of his tax returns in exchange for a $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” to compensate Americans who claim persecution by the previous administration. The point is no longer to conceal corruption but to flaunt impunity.

item.title: How China Built a Closure That Cannot Leak
item.description: Four mechanical seals, an asymmetric one-way valve, and a 2,200-year social foundation make the 2026 closure airtight in a way no previous closure has been.

item.title: ADB’s Commitment to Zero Tolerance on Reprisals Meets Uzbekistan’s Reality of Fear and Silence
item.description: Safeguards exist on paper, but are development banks prepared to confront the political realities that prevent those safeguards from functioning in practice?

item.title: What Are U.S. Military Dollars Buying in Egypt?
item.description: Israel is not the only relationship that deserves scrutiny from Congress.

item.title: Eurovision’s Troubles Mask a Deeper Problem
item.description: It’s not just the song contest that’s at risk of breaking up.

item.title: The US and China Must Learn to Coexist
item.description: The Beijing summit between Donald Trump and Xi Jinping showed that competition between the United States and China need not become a zero-sum struggle for supremacy. Even as the two countries vie for technological leadership, complete economic decoupling is neither feasible nor desirable.

item.title: The Price of Collusion: Why Sri Lanka Needs a New Approach to Debt Governance
item.description: By empowering local actors and fostering genuine competition, Sri Lanka can ensure that its debt serves the nation’s future, rather than the interests of a well-connected few.

item.title: The Gulf’s Post-OPEC Order
item.description: The United Arab Emirates’ decision to leave OPEC is the clearest expression yet of its desire to break from the Saudi-led regional order. The result could be a more fragmented Middle East marked by intensifying rivalries among Gulf powers, widening proxy conflicts, and chronic instability.

item.title: The Doublespeak of Lebanon’s Other Shiite Leader
item.description: Hezbollah’s fate will be decided by the Amal Movement’s calculations about its own future.

item.title: Why Were Shots Fired in the Philippines Senate?
item.description: The gunfight is the latest twist in a power struggle between two dynasties.

item.title: How South Asia’s Uprisings Are Reshaping Party Politics — And What Bangladesh Gets Wrong
item.description: Nepal and Sri Lanka responded to comparable uprisings by redesigning the rules of the game. Bangladesh removed the opposing team from the field and called it a victory.

item.title: Why Mexico’s Cartels Are So Hard to Defeat
item.description: The real test of Sheinbaum’s security strategy.

item.title: Iran as Vietnam, Ukraine as Korea
item.description: Similar wars end in similar ways.

item.title: 1 Year Later, Deported Bhutanese Refugees Feel the Psychological Toll of Statelessness
item.description: Bhutanese deportees in South Asia are confronting worsening mental health conditions as prolonged statelessness, family separation, and lack of legal protection deepen psychological distress.
